journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Evaluating the statistical realism of LLM-generated social science data
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) hold great promise for generating social science data, potentially expanding the methodological toolkit of quantitative social research. Prior studies have primarily focused on individual-level predictability or behavioral plausibility of LLM-generated data. We propose a framework for assessing the validity of LLM-generated data by returning to the foundational principles of survey research in the social sciences. Just as surveys based on representative samples yield statistics that approximate the corresponding statistical moments of the target population, assessment should center on the ability of LLM-generated data to reproduce real-world, population-level statistical patterns. We introduce SSDataBench, a systematic benchmark designed to evaluate population-level statistical realism in LLM-generated social science data. The benchmark assesses five types of statistical patterns central to social research: univariate distributions, bivariate associations, multivariate outcome predictions, life event sequence distributions, and associations between life event sequences and covariates. We illustrate SSDataBench using four longitudinal datasets and three cross-sectional datasets spanning six major social domains: demographics, socioeconomic status, marriage, health, abilities, and attitudes. Our study reveals representational limitations in current LLMs under sparse conditioning settings, manifested in a pronounced tendency to compress real-world heterogeneity into simplified typological structures. Finally, we outline a roadmap toward improved statistical realism and report preliminary results indicating that domain-specific training can enhance population-level realism.
discussion: Conclusion and DiscussionAI-generated social science data remain highly controversial. Despite some recent promotion of the promise of LLMs for generating synthetic data useful for social research , concerns persist as to whether such simulations meaningfully reflect real social phenomena . Before AI-generated data can be used for social inquiry, it is therefore essential to rigorously evaluate their validity.We argue that, just as the usefulness of sample-based survey data depends on their ability to reveal the statistical patterns of the real population , the validity of AI-generated synthetic data should likewise be evaluated in terms of their population-level statistical realism. Instead of focusing on individual-level predictive accuracy, assessments of AI-generated data should examine whether they reproduce statistical moments at the population-level, such as distributions, associations, and structural relationships that underpin population inference in social science.In this article, we present a systematic benchmark, SSDataBench, designed to assess whether LLM-generated data can reproduce key statistical patterns observed in real human populations. The framework evaluates five types of statistical patterns that are fundamental to social science research: univariate distributions, bivariate associations, multivariate outcome predictions, life event sequence distributions, and associations between life event sequences and covariates. We apply the benchmark framework to three cross-sectional and four longitudinal survey datasets across six major social domains using 15 LLMs. Together, these evaluations constitute a comprehensive testbed for assessing population-level statistical realism in LLM-generated social science data and provide insights into current limitations.Our results reveal several limitations of current LLMs under the evaluated setting, as they tend to generate data that accentuate typological patterns that are less true in empirical data. Specifically, LLMs struggle to reproduce real-world distributions, often collapsing toward overly typical values and failing to capture real-world heterogeneity. Second, although bivariate associations and multivariate predictions are reproduced more accurately, the models often draw on stereotyped priors, leading to associations that are frequently exaggerated. Finally, life event sequences exhibit a pronounced collapse toward normative pathways, and their association structures with other variables are misaligned with those in observed data. In short, there is a typological tendency in LLM-generated data.Despite these limitations of current LLMs under our evaluated setting, we outline several promising pathways toward improved statistical realism in future work. First, we show that richer input generally leads to better statistical realism in target variables. Second, we acknowledge the unique value of qualitative data in providing higher-dimensional information beyond structured survey-style variables and thus helping enhance simulation statistical realism. Finally, we present a preliminary exploration of domain-specific training with quantitative survey data, showing that targeted adaptation can meaningfully enhance statistical realism in social contexts and data structures beyond the training setting. These findings highlight the potential of domain-specific training to improve the realism of LLM-based social science data simulations.We recognize several limitations in our benchmark work. First, the reported pass rates inevitably involve several necessarily subjective decisions. These include the choice of the bootstrap sample size, summary statistics, the specific statistical tests and their corresponding parameterizations, and the conventional significance threshold of 0.05. In this sense, the pass rates should be interpreted comparatively rather than absolutely. For example, the relative performance patterns remain meaningful and fair across models, because all models are evaluated using the same set of statistics, tests, and thresholds. In addition, our case studies present descriptive results that complement the hypothesis testing approach. We also report the distributions of relevant statistics to provide further insight into how simulated data differ from real data beyond binary pass-fail outcomes. Moreover, our benchmark is fully configurable. Users can flexibly adjust the choice of statistics, sample sizes, tests, and significance thresholds to suit their own research objectives and assumptions.Second, in this work, we primarily evaluate LLMs under a sparse-conditioning setting, where models are asked to simulate population-level responses based solely on a limited set of demographic variables, with the target variables entirely unobserved. Under this formulation, many traditional imputation methods are not directly applicable, since they typically assume at least partial observability of the target variables. Benchmarking LLM-based simulation against state-of-the-art imputation methods in scenarios where some outcomes are partially observed remains an important avenue for future research.Third, while we use established large-scale surveys as the best available empirical baseline for evaluating population-level statistical realism, we acknowledge that they do not constitute a perfect “ground truth,” given common problems associated with survey research, including sample coverage bias, nonresponse bias, attrition bias, interviewer bias, social desirability bias, recall error, and measurement error. We further identify as an important open direction for future work the questions of whether, and under what conditions, AI models can extrapolate to hard-to-survey or underrepresented populations, as discussed in prior work .Furthermore, our preliminary experiments suggest that fine-tuning LLMs on survey data substantially improves statistical realism. Yet, we do not know if and which LLMs in our evaluation have already benefited from existing survey data. However, original survey data typically exist only in coded formats, where the numerical values themselves carry no inherent semantic meaning, and without domain-specific variable mappings such as those used in our benchmark, they are not readily useful for LLMs. The evaluation pipeline we introduce is generalizable. Researchers and institutions can adapt this benchmark to assess whether synthetic data they generate meet the standards of population-level realism required for empirical research.Looking ahead, we hope that future developments in LLM-based social simulation will explicitly integrate the measurement and preservation of population-level statistical patterns, ensuring that synthetic populations reflect the empirical statistical realism required for rigorous social science research.If, after proper training, AI-generated data can be used for social research, they hold particular promise for causal inference. As Holland pointed out four decades ago, the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference in social science is the impossibility to observe the values of outcomes associated simultaneously with treated and control conditions . If we can generate data with AI, it may be possible to generate outcomes associated with different counterfactual treatment conditions, thus overcoming the Fundamental Problem. We await future work to verify this conjecture.
figure_legends: Overview of the SSDataBench pipeline and evaluation framework. (A) Benchmark overview. The pipeline consists of three stages: data preparation, simulation generation, and evaluation. (B) Evaluation on five types of core social statistical patterns. Each type includes corresponding examples and significance tests used to determine whether statistical patterns in simulated data significantly differ from real data.

Average pass rate across models, datasets, and statistical pattern types. The heatmap reports the average pass rate for every combination of model, dataset, and statistical pattern type. Rows correspond to LLMs, and columns correspond to dataset–type combinations. Darker shading indicates higher pass rates. The average performance of each model across all dataset–type combinations is shown in parentheses next to the model name.

Case studies on NLSY using Gemini-2.5-Flash. (A) Distribution of Age at First Marriage. (B) Distribution of Self-Rated Memory. (C) Cramér’s V associations between Highest Education and other variables (x-axis). (D) Coefficient of determination (R2) values for regression models predicting various outcomes (x-axis). (E) Distributions of life event sequence of First Marriage (M), Starting Work (W), and First Childbearing (C). (F) Cramér’s V associations between life event sequence of Finishing Education (E), Starting Work (W), and First Childbearing (C) and other variables (x-axis).

Distributions of statistics across statistical pattern types. Each boxplot summarizes the distribution of values across all datasets and all evaluated variables for real and simulated populations. For each dataset and evaluated variable(s), statistics are calculated directly on the full set of simulated samples and the corresponding sampled real data, without bootstrap resampling. (A) Distributions of entropy values for categorical target variables. (B) Distributions of Cramér’s V values for paired categorical variables. (C) Distributions of the coefficient of determination R2 values for regression models. (D) Distributions of entropy values for life event sequences. (E) Distributions of Cramér’s V values between life event sequence variables and other covariates.
